content:
In this episode, Arvind Ramasamy and I explore the journey of voice #AI entrepreneurs, focusing on #CustomerOnboarding, handling pricing conversations, and the evolution of pricing models in a competitive landscape. 

If you're building an AI product or service, or are curious about #SaaS pricing strategies, this is your guide to turning interest into paying customers.

author.name: Collin Stewart
author.info: Order NOW: The Terrifying Art of Finding Customers!

postedAt.postedAgoText: 1 day ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

content:
I haven't seen your campaigns, but I'd bet some of those #OutboundEmails are super bloated because you're trying to sell before you've earned a reply.

The email I’m using right now to book #startup interviews is almost embarrassingly simple... 

Subject line: “Interview request.” 

Then: “Hey {{first_name}}, can I interview you? I’m starting a company around {{problem/category}}, and I’d love your advice.”

That’s it. 

- No fake compliment.
- No overbuilt personalization.
- No seven-line value prop pretending to be casual. 

Just a small, clear ask that feels like it came from a human instead of a sequence.

If I'm feeling frisky, I’ll add a P.S., which sometimes helps, sometimes hurts. It depends on whom you’re emailing. 

But the principle holds: if your #ColdEmail needs three paragraphs to justify the ask, the ask is probably too big.

author.name: Collin Stewart
author.info: Order NOW: The Terrifying Art of Finding Customers!

postedAt.postedAgoText: 2 days ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

content:
Most people hear “teach to sell” and assume it’s just a nicer way to pitch, but after having Dan on the show, I can assure you it’s not. 

The best sellers know you can't pressure people into buying... You can only help people understand the problem clearly enough for the next step to become obvious.

That’s why I’m excited for Dan Rochon’s book, #TeachToSell, launching today! 

It’s a much-needed push against the lazy version of #SalesAdvice: close harder, follow up more, overcome objections, force urgency.

Real sales is cleaner than that. You have to teach well, build trust, and let the right people move.

author.name: Collin Stewart
author.info: Order NOW: The Terrifying Art of Finding Customers!

postedAt.postedAgoText: 3 days ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

content:
Early revenue can make you feel like a genius, until the bill comes due.


I went from zero to seven figures in ARR in one quarter.

On paper, it looked like rocket fuel, but in reality, we moved faster than our product, our team, and our customers could handle.

We created expectations we couldn’t consistently meet. And once trust starts leaking, pipeline doesn’t save you.

Looking back, the smarter move would’ve been slower:

1. Stay closer to the service work.
2. Keep learning from customers.
3. Build the product with more discipline.
4. Earn the right to scale.

Fast growth is great, unless it teaches the market not to believe you.

Catch my full appearance on Aggaeus's podcast HERE: https://lnkd.in/dpWZHCmd

author.name: Collin Stewart
author.info: Order NOW: The Terrifying Art of Finding Customers!

postedAt.postedAgoText: 6 days ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

content:
A lot of founders want the clean version of #CustomerAcquisition.

Bad news: There is NOT one.

The real version is rough:

- You ask questions that feel too obvious.
- You pitch too early and learn why it didn't work.
- You get polite compliments that go nowhere.
- You chase the wrong segment for too long.
- You realize the thing people praise is not the thing they'll buy.

That's what I mean when I say finding customers is terrifying, it's not that sales is scary. It's just that the market tells you the truth faster than almost anything else.

You can ignore these signals all you want, but the company's bank account will feel it.

My advice: your goal should be to build a process that turns fear into learning.

author.name: Collin Stewart
author.info: Order NOW: The Terrifying Art of Finding Customers!

postedAt.postedAgoText: 1 week ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

content:
A relationship-first campaign feels slow until you understand not everyone is ready to buy right now.

- Some people are future buyers.
- Some are future partners.
- Some are future referrers.
- Some are just smart people worth knowing.

If every touchpoint asks for a meeting, you burn trust with the 95% who are not ready.

Sometimes the better play is:

- Share something useful.
- Ask a real question.
- Feature someone's thinking.
- Follow up without pretending it's urgent.
- Stay visible until timing changes.

That's compound interest on trust.

author.name: Collin Stewart
author.info: Order NOW: The Terrifying Art of Finding Customers!

postedAt.postedAgoText: 1 week ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

article.title: The Problem With Asking Everyone for a Meeting
article.subtitle: Collin Stewart
article.linkLabel: Open article: The Problem With Asking Everyone for a Meeting by Collin Stewart • 2 min read
article.description: Trust me, I know that a relationship-first approach feels painfully slow when you’re the founder staring at pipeline targets, runway, and a calendar t

content:
The first 10 customers should teach you more than they pay you.

That sounds backwards until you live it.

Early revenue is important, obviously, but the real value of those first customers is the learning:

- Why they cared.
- What almost stopped them.
- What they tried before.
- What language they used.
- What they expected after buying.
- What you misunderstood about the problem.

If you rush past that learning, you build a sales motion on top of assumptions.

That's how companies end up scaling into the wrong market, wrong message, or wrong customer.

The first customers are the curriculum.

That you can fix it with:
- More calls. 
- More emails. 
- More follow-up.

But Dan Rochon sees the problem differently. 

In our recent conversation on the #PredictableRevenuePodcast, Dan connects predictable income to something deeper than activity volume: the ability to help prospects understand their problem, trust the path forward, and make a decision without feeling pushed into it.

Check it out!

author.name: Collin Stewart
author.info: Order NOW: The Terrifying Art of Finding Customers!

postedAt.postedAgoText: 2 weeks ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

content:
I’m a HUGE Clay fan.

It might be the most powerful sales tool ever built: 

You can: 

- Find almost any company. 
- Enrich it with almost any signal.
- Score it however you want. 
- Route it into whatever workflow your team needs.

It’s ridiculous...

But after spending the last few years building outbound systems for startups, I’ve learned something the hard way:

Most founders haven’t talked to enough of the right customers to know:  

a. Who actually cares?
b. Why do they care now?
c. What signal matters?
d. What message earns attention?
e. What offer is painful enough to move money?

author.name: Collin Stewart
author.info: Order NOW: The Terrifying Art of Finding Customers!

postedAt.postedAgoText: 2 weeks ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

article.title: Clay Won’t Save You From the Work You’re Avoiding
article.subtitle: Collin Stewart
article.linkLabel: Open article: Clay Won’t Save You From the Work You’re Avoiding by Collin Stewart • 2 min read
article.description: I’m a HUGE Clay fan.It might be the most powerful sales tool ever built: You can find almost any company, enrich it with almost any signal, score it h

content:
Interviews are theater... Everyone knows the script.


1. The candidate shows confidence.
2. The hiring manager shows certainty.
3. Then, 6 months later, everyone acts surprised when the hire doesn’t work out.

Gianluca Ferremi thinks we’re measuring the wrong thing, and he’s probably right.

Technical ability is rarely the whole problem. The harder question is: How does this person behave under pressure, ambiguity, complexity, and consequences?

That’s why video games may become better hiring signals than interviews.

- No rehearsed answers.
- No polished “greatest weakness” monologue.
- No guessing what the interviewer wants to hear.

Just behavior, and behavior is the thing we should have been hiring for all along.

author.name: Collin Stewart
author.info: Order NOW: The Terrifying Art of Finding Customers!

postedAt.postedAgoText: 2 weeks ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

repost.content:
Video games might become better talent assessments than interviews and Gianluca Ferremi knows WHY:

People behave differently when they’re not trying to impress someone.

- Remove the performance.
- Remove the “right answer.”
- Add complexity, pressure, and consequences.

YOU stop getting REHEARSED responses, and THEY start getting REAL.

author.name: Hugo Estrella
author.info: Part-time Rapper, Marketing Coordinator, Content and Art Enthusiast

postedAt.postedAgoText: 1 month ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

content:
Cold email is NOT dead, but generic timing is.


A lot of founders write outbound like every person on the list is ready to buy this week, and guess what? They aren't.

Most are running their business, dealing with other fires, and not thinking about you at all. They probably don't even know you exist.

That means the old "clean value prop + calendar link" email only works for the tiny slice of people already in-market. For everyone else, it feels like noise.

---

Before you send, answer two questions:

1. Why now? 
Can you point to something specific that makes this week relevant?

2. Why them?
If they forwarded your email to their boss, would the boss agree it's their problem to fix?

If you can't answer both, DON'T send that email. It's still a draft.

author.name: Collin Stewart
author.info: Order NOW: The Terrifying Art of Finding Customers!

postedAt.postedAgoText: 3 weeks ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

content:
I used to think “non-scalable” was startup code for “we don’t have a real growth motion yet.”

I’m less sure now... After talking with Ankur Patel from Multimodal, I think some markets require the non-scalable stuff first.

- Advisory boards. 
- Trust-building. 
- Peer conversations. 
- Actual time with the people you want to serve.

Especially in #AI, where everyone is suddenly competing with #ChatGPT, #Claude, #Microsoft, and 500 other vendors saying some version of the same thing.

The product still has to be great. But in a high-trust market, being better isn’t always enough. 

Buyers need to believe you understand their world, their risk, and the path to adopting the technology safely.

That’s the part founders love to skip. It’s also the part that might get you the right to show the product in the first place.

author.name: Collin Stewart
author.info: Order NOW: The Terrifying Art of Finding Customers!

postedAt.postedAgoText: 3 weeks ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

content:
The most dangerous feedback a founder can get: "That's really smart."


Yes... It feels good, it sounds validating, and it makes the deck feel less ridiculous.

But it does not tell you if someone will buy or not.

In the early days, I treated positive feedback like traction... I heard interest and assumed demand.

WRONG.

A better question is: What are they already doing to solve this? If the answer is "nothing," you're probably not looking at a painful problem yet.

If the answer is "spreadsheets, duct tape, consultants, interns, late nights, bad tools, and still not enough," now you're getting closer.

The Terrifying Art of Finding Customers is the book I wish I had when I was confusing compliments with traction.

If you're in that stage, it will save you some scars. Don't take it from me! Ask Lars Nilsson or Kris Rudeegraap... they'll vouch.
